attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic concerned about a lump on the roof of her mouth that she noticed while brushing her teeth two weeks ago. She reports no pain, bleeding, or changes in size. She has a medical history of hypertension and osteoarthritis. Her medications include amlodipine and paracetamol as needed. She does not smoke or consume alcohol.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Intraoral examination reveals a firm, non-tender, midline bony protuberance on the hard palate covered by normal mucosa. The lesion measures approximately 2 cm in diameter and has a smooth surface (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: CT scan of the head and neck
Referral to oral and maxillofacial surgery
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation described is consistent with torus palatinus, which is a benign bony growth (exostosis) that occurs on the hard palate. Torus palatinus is a common anatomical variant found in approximately 20-30% of a Western population, with higher prevalence among those of Asian ethnicity, particularly women. It typically presents as a firm, non-tender midline protuberance on the hard palate covered by normal mucosa, exactly as described in this case.

Reassurance and no further intervention is the most appropriate management for torus palatinus. These lesions are benign, slow-growing, and typically asymptomatic. They do not undergo malignant transformation and require no treatment unless they interfere with denture fitting, cause recurrent traumatic ulceration, or interfere with speech or swallowing. In this case, the patient has no pain or complicating symptoms with her torus palatinus, and can be reassured.

Referral to oral and maxillofacial surgery would be unnecessary for a typical torus palatinus. Surgical removal is only indicated if the torus causes functional problems, interferes with denture placement, or if there is diagnostic uncertainty requiring histopathological examination.

Incisional biopsy is not indicated for a lesion with classic clinical features of torus palatinus. The characteristic appearance, location, and consistency of the lesion make the diagnosis straightforward in most cases. Biopsy would be considered if there were atypical features such as rapid growth, ulceration, or asymmetry that raised suspicion for malignancy.

Antibiotics would be appropriate for conditions such as a palatal abscess, which would present with pain, erythema, and possibly fluctuance. The absence of pain, normal mucosal appearance, and firm consistency make an infectious process unlikely in this case.

CT scan of the head and neck is not necessary for diagnosis of a typical torus palatinus. The clinical examination is usually sufficient. Imaging might be considered if there were concerns about the extent of the lesion prior to surgical removal, or if there were features suggesting a different diagnosis such as a salivary gland tumour or malignancy.

The differential diagnosis for palatal lesions includes palatal abscess, salivary gland tumors, squamous cell carcinoma, and other bony lesions such as osteomas. However, the classic presentation of a firm, midline, non-tender bony protuberance covered by normal mucosa is highly specific for torus palatinus.
learningPoints: Torus palatinus is a benign bony growth on the hard palate that requires no treatment unless it interferes with denture fitting or causes functional problems.
Torus palatinus is present in about 20-30% of the general Western population, and may be more prevalent in Asian populations.
Differential diagnosis of palatal lesions includes torus palatinus, palatal abscess, salivary gland tumors, and squamous cell carcinoma.
references: Loh, K. Y., Ong, B. E., & Elango, S. (2009). A mass on the palate. Australian family physician, 38(1-2), 47. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/272332ab-fd60-45f0-b5df-a5269eb27b9c/A-mass-on-the-palate.aspx#:~:text=Torus%20palatinus%20is%20a%20benign,at%20the%20median%20palatine%20suture.&text=Patients%20need%20reassurance%20as%20this,condition%20and%20majority%20are%20asymptomatic.
